summary: Cathy O'Neil argues that the Federal Reserve's quantitative easing program ($3.7 trillion total) did cause inflation, but only for the wealthy investor class rather than everyday goods. While conventional inflation metrics remained stable, asset prices like stocks, New York apartments, and high-end art skyrocketed, making the rich dramatically richer. She notes the irony that billionaire Paul Singer is now complaining about how expensive the Hamptons have become. The post suggests this 'hyperinflation for the rich' has widened the gap between the wealthy and average Americans, with much of this wealth increasingly coming from inheritance rather than entrepreneurial success.
key_points: QE pumped $3.7 trillion into the economy - comparable to the entire federal budget - yet conventional inflation remained stable
Standard inflation calculations miss asset price inflation because they focus on median consumer goods, not investment portfolios
The stock market hitting all-time highs represents inflation for the investor class, letting them outbid others for luxury goods and real estate
Even billionaires like Paul Singer are complaining about 'hyperinflation' in their world - Hamptons real estate and high-end art
The growing wealth concentration is increasingly driven by inherited wealth rather than entrepreneurial success
money_quotes: My argument is that it indeed caused inflation, but only for the rich, where by rich I mean investor class.
The stock market is at an all time high, and rich people are way richer, and that doesn't matter for any inflation calculation because the median income is flat.
It's 'hyperinflation for the rich' and we are not feeling very bad for them.
In fact it has made matters worse, when the very rich have even less in common with the average person.
Just in case you're thinking, oh well, all those Steve Jobs types deserve their hyper-inflated success, keep in mind that more and more of the people we're talking about come from inherited wealth.
We really don't know exactly what happened because of all this money, because we can't go back in time and do without the QE's.
key_insight: Quantitative easing created a hidden form of inflation that only affects the wealthy, invisible to standard metrics but profoundly widening inequality.
